---
title: "Install jstats"
subtitle: "Getting the package onto your computer — the current GitHub-phase steps, plus what to do if an install stalls."
---

This page gets the jstats package installed and loaded, ready to use. It assumes
you've already installed R and RStudio and have an RStudio Project open. If you
haven't, start with [Install R and RStudio](install-r-and-rstudio.qmd) and
[RStudio Orientation](rstudio-orientation.qmd), then come back here.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="For experienced R users"}
If you've installed R packages before, only a few things here are specific to
jstats. It currently installs from **GitHub** rather than CRAN, so you need the
**remotes** package and — on Windows — **Rtools**; and it checks for a newer
version each time it loads. Jump to the two install commands below; the rest is
ordinary install-and-load you already know.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
jstats is currently distributed through **GitHub** — a website where software is
shared and developed. Later it will also be on **CRAN** (the same archive you
downloaded R from), at which point installing it gets simpler. These instructions
cover the current GitHub phase. You install once; after that, the package tells
you whenever an update is available (see *Keep jstats up to date*, below).
:::

Using any R package is really two steps that are easy to blur together.
**Installing** downloads the package onto your computer — you do this once.
**Loading** switches it on for your current session — you do this each time you
start R, with `library()`. (Think of installing as buying a book for your shelf,
and loading as taking it down to read.) jstats works the same way: the steps
below do the one-time install, and the last section sets up the loading to happen
on its own.

## What you need first

- **R and RStudio installed** — see [Install R and RStudio](install-r-and-rstudio.qmd).
- **An RStudio Project open** — [RStudio Orientation](rstudio-orientation.qmd)
  covers creating one. The automatic-loading step at the end of this page uses a
  small file that lives *inside* your Project, so it helps to have one ready.

## Install Rtools (Windows only)

To install a package from GitHub, Windows needs **Rtools**, a free build toolkit.
Download it from <https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/>, choose the
version that matches your R (for example **Rtools45** for R 4.5.x), and run the
installer with its defaults. You do this once, unless you update R later.

(On a Mac you don't need Rtools — but you may need Apple's command line tools; see
the Mac note just below the install commands.)

## Install the remotes helper

The **remotes** package is what handles installing from GitHub. Click in the
**Console** (bottom-left pane) and run the line below. This is a one-off command
you won't need to save in a script:

```r
install.packages("remotes")
```

## Install jstats and load it

Now install jstats from GitHub, then load it:

```r
remotes::install_github("JMA61/jstats")
library(jstats)
```

If it loaded cleanly, you'll see a short jstats startup message — and, if a newer
version is already out, a note with the exact lines to update (more on that
below). That message is how you know jstats is loaded and ready.

**Mac users.** You may already have the build tools you need. If you hit a build
error during installation, open the **Terminal** app and run:

```
xcode-select --install
```

Follow the prompts, then run the install command again. The `remotes` and install
lines are the same as above.

### If the install fails

Most install problems are one of a handful of things. Scan the titles below and
open the one that matches your error. If yours isn't here, the full
[Troubleshooting](troubleshooting.qmd) page has more.

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="API rate limit exceeded"}
GitHub limits how many requests it answers from one internet connection per hour.
A single install is well under the limit, but two situations can trip it: retrying
a failing install many times in quick succession, or several people on the **same
network** (a campus or office connection) all installing within the same hour and
hitting the shared limit together. The fix is simply to **wait an hour and try
again**, and to avoid rapid repeated retries. This limit goes away entirely once
jstats reaches CRAN.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="there is no package called 'remotes'"}
The install-from-GitHub command needs the **remotes** package, and it isn't
installed yet. Run this first, then retry the install:

```r
install.packages("remotes")
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="A build or compilation error"}
On **Windows**, this usually means **Rtools** is missing or doesn't match your R
version. Install the matching Rtools (see *Install Rtools*, above), then
**restart RStudio** (Session menu, Restart R) and try the install again. On a
**Mac**, open Terminal and run `xcode-select --install`, follow the prompts, then
retry.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="The install seems half-done or broken"}
The simplest fix is to remove jstats and install it fresh:

```r
remove.packages("jstats")
remotes::install_github("JMA61/jstats")
library(jstats)
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="RStudio hangs or loops every time it starts"}
This almost always means an **install command was put in your `.Rprofile`**. That
file runs *every time R starts*, so an install line tries to reinstall on every
startup and can loop. Your `.Rprofile` should only ever hold lightweight startup
lines like `library(jstats)` — never an install command. To recover: close
RStudio, open your Project folder in your computer's file manager, open the
`.Rprofile` file in a plain text editor (Notepad on Windows, TextEdit on Mac),
delete the install line so only the `library(jstats)` line remains, save, and
reopen RStudio.
:::

## Keep jstats up to date

Because jstats is updated often during this phase, it checks for a newer version
each time you load it with `library(jstats)`. When one is available, it prints a
short message with a few lines of code — you simply copy those lines into the
Console and run them, and jstats updates itself. You don't have to track releases
or hunt anything down; just watch for the message when the package loads and copy
what it gives you. (This all gets simpler once jstats reaches CRAN; we'll update
these instructions when it does.)

::: {.callout-important title="This is a beta, and your input shapes it"}
While jstats is in this pre-release phase, it's still actively growing — which
makes now the best time to ask for things. If a function is missing an option
your work needs, or you'd like an analysis it doesn't yet cover, say so: open an
issue on the package's GitHub page (the built-in spot for reporting problems and
requesting features). If you hit an error, paste the **full error message** into
the issue, along with what you ran and which versions of R and jstats you're on —
that detail makes a fix far quicker. Changes are far easier to fold in now than
after the package reaches its stable release, where keeping existing code working
limits what can change.
:::

## Load jstats automatically (recommended)

You can tell your Project to load jstats for you every time it opens, so you never
have to type `library(jstats)` again. It's optional, but worth doing: besides
saving the keystrokes, it means the package's automatic update check runs every
time you start work — so you're always told when a new version is available — and
you open each session ready to go. This uses a small setup file called an
**`.Rprofile`** (a file R reads automatically when it starts).

Create a plain text file named `.Rprofile` in your Project folder containing one
line:

```r
library(jstats)
```

Save it, then restart R (**Session menu, Restart R**). From now on, every session
in this Project starts with jstats already loaded.

Keep this to lightweight startup lines — loading a package, and later a setting or
two. Do **not** put an install command (like `remotes::install_github(...)`) in
`.Rprofile`: because the file runs on every startup, an install line there tries
to reinstall each time and can send RStudio into a loop. Installing is a one-off
you run by hand; loading is the repeatable step that belongs in startup.

We're keeping this minimal for now. Later you'll add a line or two more — where
your data lives, and how many digits to show in output — but one line is plenty to
start.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Going deeper (optional)"}
This `.Rprofile` lives inside your Project and applies only to this Project.
That's deliberate — it's self-contained and safe to edit. There's also a global,
all-Projects version, but it's better left until you're juggling several Projects
at once; the books cover it.
:::
